threats.title: AccessControlHooks onQueueWithdrawal missing isHooked check allows unauthenticated credential burning
threats.hypothesis_description: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal lacks the `isHooked` validation check present in other hooks. Any external address can call this function, passing a victim's address and malicious `hooksData`. This triggers stateful credential validation on role providers, potentially burning the victim's one-time-use credentials or altering their access state without their consent.
threats.investigation_rationale: The missing `isHooked` check breaks the trust boundary, allowing arbitrary callers to invoke the hook logic as if they were a legitimate market contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing `if (!market.isHooked) revert NotHookedMarket();` check before processing lender status

preconditions: Target role provider uses stateful validation (e.g., burns one-time tokens)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any address can call the hook and trigger stateful provider validation

analysis_questions: Can an attacker burn one-time credentials?
Can this be used to DoS lenders by invalidating their state?
validation_requirements: Test calling onQueueWithdrawal from non-market address
Verify provider state changes during unauthorized call

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls onQueueWithdrawal with victim's address and credential in hooksData, burning the credential without a real withdrawal.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of access for lenders, potential DoS

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for unauthenticated calls to onQueueWithdrawal

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain visibility of unauthorized hook calls

threats.title: HooksConfig.onBorrow uses incorrect base size constant for calldata assembly
threats.hypothesis_description: HooksConfig.onBorrow uses `RepayHook_Base_Size` instead of `BorrowHook_Base_Size` when calculating the calldata size for the external hook call. While both constants currently share the same value (0x0224), any future modification to one constant without the other will cause memory corruption or out-of-bounds calldata construction.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using the wrong constant in assembly memory operations is a clear code defect that creates a severe maintenance hazard and potential future exploit vector.

proof_of_signal.pattern: let size := add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes) inside onBorrow

preconditions: Constants diverge in future updates or derived contracts

analysis_questions: Are there any edge cases where the sizes currently differ?
Does this affect any derived or inherited implementations?
validation_requirements: Check for other copy-paste errors in assembly blocks

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If constants diverge, calldata for onBorrow hook will be incorrectly sized, leading to reverts or reading garbage data.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None currently, potential DoS in future

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing strict calldata size validation tests

threats.title: Reentrancy via _tryValidateCredential stateful call to untrusted role providers
threats.hypothesis_description: _tryValidateCredential uses a stateful `call` to external role providers. If a borrower adds a malicious role provider, or if a provider is compromised, it can reenter the market contract during the hook execution (e.g., during onDeposit or onTransfer) before the market state is fully updated.
threats.investigation_rationale: External calls to semi-trusted contracts without reentrancy guards in the calling context are a classic reentrancy vector.
preconditions: Market contract lacks reentrancy guards on the calling function

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Stateful call to provider can reenter market

analysis_questions: Does the market contract have reentrancy guards?
What state is vulnerable during the hook callback?
validation_requirements: Trace complete execution path for reentrancy
Test attack feasibility with malicious provider contract

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Malicious provider reenters market.deposit() during the credential validation step, inflating balances before the first deposit completes.
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing reentrancy tests for provider callbacks

threats.title: Gas Griefing / DoS via _tryGetCredential staticcall to untrusted role providers
threats.hypothesis_description: _tryGetCredential uses `staticcall(gas(), ...)` passing all available gas to the external provider. A malicious or buggy pull provider can enter an infinite loop, consuming all gas and causing an Out of Gas revert, effectively DoSing the market's deposit and transfer functions for all users.
threats.investigation_rationale: Passing all gas to an external contract allows it to grief the transaction. A gas limit (e.g., 50k) should be used for credential checks.
preconditions: Malicious or buggy role provider added by borrower

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded gas passed to external call
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Provider can consume all gas and revert parent tx

analysis_questions: Does this block critical protocol operations?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Provider enters infinite loop, consuming all gas. Lenders cannot deposit or transfer tokens.

threats.title: Shared _lenderStatus across multiple markets allows unintended credential reuse
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_lenderStatus` mapping is global to the hooks contract, not keyed by market address. If a borrower deploys multiple markets using the same hooks contract instance, a credential granted for Market A will automatically grant access to Market B, bypassing market-specific access policies.
threats.investigation_rationale: Access control state should typically be isolated per market unless explicitly designed as a shared whitelist.
deployment_assumptions: Borrower reuses the same hooks contract instance for multiple markets with different intended access policies
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence provided, but architecture allows hook reuse
preconditions: Multiple markets share the same hooks contract

analysis_questions: Can a borrower enforce separate policies?
validation_requirements: Deploy two markets with same hooks

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Lender gets approved for low-risk Market A, then uses the same credential to deposit into restricted Market B.

threats.title: MarketState.withdrawableProtocolFees underflow causes DoS on fee collection
threats.hypothesis_description: withdrawableProtocolFees calculates `totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals`. If the market is undercollateralized (e.g., due to bad debt) and `totalAssets` drops below `normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals`, this subtraction will underflow and revert, preventing the protocol from collecting any fees.
threats.investigation_rationale: Arithmetic underflow in view/pure functions can DoS state-changing transactions if called internally during fee collection.
preconditions: Fee collection function calls this internally

analysis_questions: Does fee collection call this function?
Can totalAssets drop below unclaimed withdrawals?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Market becomes undercollateralized. Protocol attempts to collect fees, but transaction reverts due to underflow.

threats.title: Borrower can bypass temporary reserve ratio penalty via step-down APR reductions
threats.hypothesis_description: MarketConstraintHooks applies a temporary reserve ratio penalty only if the APR reduction exceeds 25%. A borrower can repeatedly reduce the APR by exactly 25%, wait for the 2-week expiry to clear the temporary state, and reduce again. This allows dropping the APR to near zero over time without ever triggering the protective reserve ratio increase.
threats.investigation_rationale: The economic protection mechanism can be systematically bypassed through scheduled, incremental parameter changes.
preconditions: Borrower executes APR reductions in <= 25% increments spaced 2 weeks apart

analysis_questions: Is this gradual reduction intended behavior?
Does it expose lenders to undue risk?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Borrower drops APR from 100% to 1% over 8 weeks without ever holding extra reserves.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of yield and reserve protection for lenders

threats.title: FixedTermLoanHooks allows borrower to manipulate term by setting it to 0
threats.hypothesis_description: The borrower can call `setFixedTermEndTime` with `newFixedTermEndTime = 0`, immediately ending the fixed term. Lenders who deposited expecting a locked term might have their expectations violated, and the borrower can use this to manipulate withdrawal queues unexpectedly after deposits are made.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the borrower is trusted, the ability to instantly remove the core protection of a 'fixed term' loan product undermines the protocol's guarantees to lenders.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (newFixedTermEndTime > hookedMarket.fixedTermEndTime) revert IncreaseFixedTerm(); (no lower bound check)

preconditions: Borrower acts maliciously or unpredictably against lender interests

analysis_questions: Can this be used to front-run lender actions?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Borrower advertises 1-year lockup, gathers deposits, then sets term to 0 to manipulate market dynamics.
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for extreme term reductions
